summary: The first part of the problem has been resolved: Berman, Chappell, Faudree, Gimbel, Hartman, and Williams proved that the icosahedron has obstacle number 2, showing that planar graphs can indeed have obstacle number greater than 1. However, the second question — whether there exists a constant $k$ such that every planar graph has obstacle number at most $k$ — remains open; the best known upper bound for an $n$-vertex planar graph is $O(n)$.

since_posted.title: Graphs with Obstacle Number Greater than One
since_posted.authors: Leah Wrenn Berman, Glenn G. Chappell, Jill R. Faudree, John Gimbel, Chris Hartman, Gordon I. Williams
since_posted.venue: Journal of Graph Algorithms and Applications
since_posted.claim: Proves that the icosahedron has obstacle number exactly 2 (using a SAT-solver-assisted proof), answering the question of Alpert, Koch, and Laison and showing that planar graphs are not all representable with a single obstacle; also shows the gyroelongated 4-bipyramid (order 10) has obstacle number 2.

since_posted.title: Obstacle Numbers of Planar Graphs
since_posted.authors: John Gimbel, Patrice Ossona de Mendez, Pavel Valtr
since_posted.claim: Establishes that the maximum planar obstacle number (non-crossing visibility representation) of an $n$-vertex planar graph is $n-3$, and proves that every bipartite planar graph of order at least 3 has (standard) obstacle number 1.

since_posted.title: Bounding and Computing Obstacle Numbers of Graphs
since_posted.authors: Martin Balko, Steven Chaplick, Robert Ganian, Siddharth Gupta, Michael Hoffmann, Pavel Valtr, Alexander Wolff
since_posted.claim: Improves general lower bounds on obstacle number (to $\Omega(n/\log\log n)$ for simple polygon obstacles and $\Omega(n)$ for convex obstacles) and shows FPT algorithms for obstacle number parameterized by vertex cover, but no new constant bound for planar graphs.

search_queries: obstacle number planar graphs upper bound
obstacle number graphs arXiv conjecture planar 2012 2020
Alpert Koch Laison obstacle number planar graph icosahedron dodecahedron
"obstacle number" planar graph greater than 1 solved proved
notes: The two-part problem is partially resolved: the first question (existence of a planar graph with obstacle number > 1) was answered affirmatively by Berman et al. 2017 via computer-assisted proof. The second question (existence of a universal constant bound k for all planar graphs) remains fully open as of 2026; the patmorin survey page notes it is 'plausible that planar graphs have bounded obstacle number' but no proof exists. The Gimbel et al. paper (1706.06992) mainly studies the planar obstacle number variant (non-crossing drawings), which is a different (and harder) variant; its n-3 upper bound does not directly answer the constant-bound question for standard obstacle numbers. The Springer chapter version of the Gimbel paper (WG 2017 proceedings) could not be fetched due to authentication redirect.
